narrative: As a Black male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, focusing on factors like academic records, test scores, and background to forecast first-time pass rates. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 3.00, LSAT score of 27, Year 1 school decile of 2nd out of 10, Year 3 decile of 1st out of 10, middle family income bracket, and full-time student status—and predicted a 69.9% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This high predicted failure risk is why your application was rejected, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to succeed on the bar to maintain strong outcomes. The most influential factor pushing the model toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 academic decile, which placed you in the bottom 10% of your school that year—far below the typical student's middle-of-the-pack standing—and signaled a concerning drop in performance over time. Next in importance, your undergraduate GPA of 3.00 worked in your favor by pulling the prediction slightly toward passing, as it's close to the average of 3.21 across applicants, showing solid prior achievement. Your Year 1 decile of 2nd out of 10 also nudged the odds toward failure, being well below the average 6th decile, though less strongly than your later grades. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your LSAT of 27 is notably lower than the average 37, and your early low deciles suggest challenges in keeping pace academically that outweighed strengths like your full-time commitment and middle-income background. While factors like your race and gender were part of the full profile, the model zeroed in on these academic trends as the biggest drivers. This doesn't define your potential—many students overcome similar hurdles. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score toward the average, strengthening your later-year grades through targeted coursework or tutoring, and perhaps gaining practical experience like internships to build a stronger narrative for future applications. Reapplying after addressing these gaps could shift the model's view in your favor, opening doors to law school. You're clearly driven to pursue this path, and with focused improvements, success on the bar and beyond is within reach.
